coredata.dc:title: A smartphone-adaptable dual-signal readout chemosensor for rapid detection of nitrite in food samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jianhua
Yang, Jingying
Dong, Chuan
Wang, Yu
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: A simple 7-nitrobenzofurazan derivative, NBDA, was easily synthesized through a one-step reaction of NBD-Cl and o-phenylenediamine. NBDA was utilized as a colorimetric and fluorogenic probe for rapid, selective and sensitive sensing of nitrite. Once NO2 - ions was added to the NBDA solution at pH 1.0, the solution color changed from yellow to colorless and meanwhile the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm was quickly enhanced (ca. 43fold). These changes are due to the reaction of NBDA with NO2 - under the acidic condition to yield a benzotriazole product, which suppressed both photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and intra-molecular charge transfer (ICT) processes. The detection limits of NBDA for NO2 - from fluorescence and ratiometric absorption spectra were 4.921 × 10−8 M and 5.83 × 10−9 M, respectively. NBDA has been successfully applied to the analysis of nitrite in ham and pickle samples with recoveries in the range of 96.13–108.19 %. Paper strips loaded with NBDA were fabricated as a convenient and efficient NO2 - test kit. Moreover, a smartphone-based sensing platform by colorimetry was established, which is convenient for real-time and on-site monitoring of NO2 -.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemosensor
Nitrite
Detection
Food samples
Smartphone

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent sensing of NO2 -

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are thankful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and No. 22274090 ) and the Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Changes in the spectra of NBDA upon addition of various anions (10 equiv.): (a) absorption spectra; (b) emission spectra; (c) fluorescence intensities of NBDA (4 μM) at 520 nm upon addition of NO2 - in the presence of interfered ions. Inset: visual changes observed for NBDA in the presence of various anions under daylight and a UV-lamp of 365 nm.

Effect of pH and response time: (a) the emission intensities of NBDA in the absence and presence of NO2 - in DMF/water (v/v, 4:1) at different pH values; (b) time-dependent fluorescence intensity change of NBDA before and after adding NO2 - in DMF/HCl (v/v, 4:1, pH=1).

Changes in the spectra of NBDA (10 μM) after adding different concentrations of NO2 -: (a) UV–vis absorption spectra; (b) emission spectra; (c) A linear relationship of the absorbance ratio (A390 nm/A477 nm) values versus [NO2 -]; (d) The fluorescence intensity at 520 nm vs NO2 - concentrations (0–96 μM). Red line：the linear relation of fluorescence intensity to the concentration of NO2 - (0–64 μM).
1H NMR spectra of NBDA in the presence of NaNO2 in d6-DMSO: (a) NBDA only; (b) NBDA + NO2 - (2.0 equiv.).
ESI-HRMS of NBDA upon addition of NO2 - in CH3CN.

The detection of NO2 - based on a smartphone sensing platform: the schematic illustration of the sensing platform (a); Photo of the NBDA-loaded paper strips upon treatment with different concentration of NO2 - ions under visible and UV light (365 nm) (b); A linear relationship of R/(R+G+B) values versus [NO2 -] based on the daylight images (c) and a linear relationship of R/(R+G+B) values versus [NO2 -] based on the fluorescence images (d).
Synthesis of NBDA (a) and the sensing mechanism of NBDA for NO2 - (b).
Performance comparison of NBDA with some recently reported NO2 - sensors.
Quantitative analysis of NaNO2 by using NBDA and the spectrophotometric method.

head.ce:title: A smartphone-adaptable dual-signal readout chemosensor for rapid detection of nitrite in food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Environmental Science and Engineering Research, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple 7-nitrobenzofurazan derivative, NBDA, was easily synthesized through a one-step reaction of NBD-Cl and o-phenylenediamine. NBDA was utilized as a colorimetric and fluorogenic probe for rapid, selective and sensitive sensing of nitrite. Once NO2 - ions was added to the NBDA solution at pH 1.0, the solution color changed from yellow to colorless and meanwhile the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm was quickly enhanced (ca. 43fold). These changes are due to the reaction of NBDA with NO2 - under the acidic condition to yield a benzotriazole product, which suppressed both photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and intra-molecular charge transfer (ICT) processes. The detection limits of NBDA for NO2 - from fluorescence and ratiometric absorption spectra were 4.921 × 10−8 M and 5.83 × 10−9 M, respectively. NBDA has been successfully applied to the analysis of nitrite in ham and pickle samples with recoveries in the range of 96.13–108.19 %. Paper strips loaded with NBDA were fabricated as a convenient and efficient NO2 - test kit. Moreover, a smartphone-based sensing platform by colorimetry was established, which is convenient for real-time and on-site monitoring of NO2 -.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most universal nitrogen compounds in the nature, nitrite has been widely used in many different industries, for example, as fertilizing agent in agriculture and as additive and preservative agents in food processing industry (Moorcroft et al., 2011; Gomes et al., 2006; Møller and Skibsted, 2002). On the other hand, nitrite is a potential toxic substance (Lewis and Morris, 1986; Cammack et al., 1999) and should therefore be used with great caution. NO2 - can interact with secondary amines and amides in food products and within the human digestive system, forming carcinogenic N-nitrosamines and probably increasing the risk of cancer and other serious diseases (Wolff and Wasserman, 1972; Kilfoy et al., 2011; Shpaizer et al., 2018). In addition, high concentration of nitrite in the body can interfere with the oxygen-carrying capacity of hemoglobin and may cause methemoglobinemia, a blood disorder. Due to its great adverse effects on public health and the environment, the World Health Organization and the US Environmental Protection Agency have respectively prescribed the maximum limit of 3.0 mg/L (65.2 μM) and 1.0 mg/L (21.7 μM) for nitrite in drinking water (WHO, 2008). Consequently, fast and accurate on-site monitoring of nitrites is very important for environment protection and food quality and safety control.

So far, multiple analytical approaches have been employed for detecting nitrites, such as chromatography (Li et al., 2000; He et al., 2011a, 2011b), spectrophotometry (Daniel et al., 2009a, 2009b; Lin et al., 2011), electrochemical (Zhang et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2018c; Kung et al., 2015), capillary electrophoresis (He et al., 2011a, 2011b; Wang et al., 2017), and fluorimetry (Li et al., 2018; Xiang et al., 2018). Among them the fluorescence-based detection has received considerable attention as a promising method, owing to the advantages of low cost, simple handling, low detection limit and high selectivity (Wang et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2016; Gu et al., 2016). In the past few years, various reactive nitrite fluorescent probes have been developed with nitrite ion as a reagent or as a catalyst for chemical reactions (Zhan et al., 2017; Cai et al., 2017; Dai et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2018c; Wu et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2021; He et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2021). Nevertheless, these probes often suffer from some defects like complex synthesis, low luminescence quantum efficiency, "turn-off" fluorescence response with low sensitivity and long response time (Zhan et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2018c; Wu et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2021; He et al., 2020). Given this, there is still much room for improvement and developing simple yet effective fluorescent turn-on probes for rapid and sensitive detection of nitrite is still a research focus.

4-Chloro-7-nitro-1,2,3-benzoxadiazole (NBD-Cl) is a favorable fluorophore for its large molar absorption coefficient, visible-light absorption and emission, good light stability, and easy modification (Lee et al., 2017). Taking advantage of the reaction between ortho-phenylenediamine with NO2 - under the acidic condition, in this work we developed a simple NBD-based colorimetric and fluorescent probe, NBDA, for rapid and specific detection of nitrite. The probe was readily synthesized in one step as illustrated in Scheme 1a. As reported in the literature (Wu et al., 2019), o-diamine of the probe reacted with nitrite in an acidic medium to yield benzotriazole moiety, which induced suppression of both PET and ICT processes (Scheme 1b). As a result, significant fluorescence enhancement was observed, accompanied with an evident solution color change. The proposed method is simple, convenient, and has been successfully applied to the analysis of nitrite in food samples with good precision and accuracy. Moreover, a smartphone-based sensing platform by colorimetry was established for on-site quantitation of NO2 -.
All the reagents and solvents were of analytical reagent grade and were obtained from commercial sources and used as received. Details are provided in the Supporting Information.

In a 50 mL three-necked reaction flask, 0.1 g (0.5 mmol) NBD-Cl was dissolved in 6 mL ethanol, and then a solution of 1,2-diaminobenzene (0.1 g, 0.5 mmol) in 6 mL ethanol was added. After being stirred at reflux for 8 h, the precipitate was filtered off, rinsed with ethyl acetate to obtain the target product (NBDA) as a red solid (Scheme 1a). Yield = 43.0 % (0.058 g). 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO-d6), δ (ppm): 10.701(s, 1H, -NH), 8.533(d, 1H, ArH), 7.119(m, 2H, ArH), 6.829(d,1H, ArH), 6.635(t,1H, ArH), 5.915(d, 1H, ArH), 5.279(s, 2H, -NH2). 13C NMR (DMSO-d6): δ 102.12, 116.23, 116.50, 121.24, 122.17, 127.98, 129.26, 138.45, 144.72, 144.82, 145.26. HRMS (ESI): calcd. for C12H9N5O [M-H]- m/z 270.0626, found 270.0639. (1H, 13C NMR and ESI-MS spectra are presented in Fig. S2-S4).

A 1 × 10−3 M stock solution of NBDA was prepared in DMSO. The solutions of various testing species (1 × 10−2 M) were prepared from NaNO3, Na2SO3, NaS2, NaHSO3, Na2SO4, NaHSO4, NaHCO3, Na2CO3, NaH2PO4, KCN and NaNO2 in deionized water. Test solutions were prepared by mixing a certain volume of NBDA and each analyte stock solution in a 5.0 mL volumetric flask, followed by dilution to 5.0 mL with N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF)/0.1 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) (v/v = 4:1, pH 1.0). The resulting solution was shaken well and kept for 10 min at room temperature before recording the absorption and fluorescence spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent sensing of NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para: The optical responses of NBDA to nitrite were investigated in DMF/0.1 M HCl solution (v/v = 4:1, pH 1.0). As presented in Fig. 1a, NBDA shows a strong absorption peak at 477 nm attributable to the ICT transition, owing to the strong electron-withdrawing capability of nitro group (Zhang et al., 2019). Besides, NBDA exhibited very weak emission at 520 nm (Fig. 1b) due to an efficient PET process from the electron-rich phenyl diamine to the excited NBD fluorophore (Daniel et al., 2009a, 2009b). Upon addition of 10 equiv. of different anions (NO2 -, SO3 2-, HSO3 -, SO4 2-, HSO4 -, NO3 -, I-, Cl-, Br-, F-, HCO3 -, CO3 2-, H2PO4 -, CN-), only NO2 - induced an apparent blue shift (87 nm) in the absorption maximum of NBDA accompanied with a distinct visual color change from yellow to colorless (Fig. 1a, inset), indicating that the ICT effect was effectively weakened. Concomitantly, among the ions investigated, only addition of NO2 - resulted in a significant fluorescence enhancement of NBDA (ca. 43fold). Obviously, the green fluorescence of NBDA was switched from a dark background to the “on” state upon reaction with NO2 - (Fig. 1b, inset), allowing naked eye detection of NO2 - under a ultra violet (UV) lamp. The observed spectroscopic changes are presumably due to the reaction of NBDA and NO2 - under the acidic condition to yield a benzotriazole product, which suppressed both the PET and ICT processes (Scheme 1b).

To evaluate the anti-interference ability of NBDA for detecting NO2 -, we also performed competitive experiments by adding other potentially competing species into the tested NO2 - solutions. As manifested in Fig. 1c, the presence of coexisting anions had a negligible impact on the fluorescence response of NBDA toward NO2 -. Therefore, NBDA could effectively detect NO2 - ions with high specificity and selectivity.

To achieve optimal sensing performance, influence of pH of the medium on the fluorescence response of NBDA to NO2 - was studied in DMF/H2O (v/v = 4/1) solution. Fig. 2a shows the fluorescence intensity of NBDA remained unchanged in the pH range of 1.0–7.0, revealing its high stability over a wide pH range. After adding NO2 - (6 equiv.), significant emission enhancement was found at low pH values (pH < 3.0), whereas the emission intensity of NBDA was not altered at higher pH values since the reaction between ortho-aromatic diamines and nitrite takes place in acidic media (Scheme 1b). So, all measurements were done in 0.1 M HCl solution to realize a rapid and sensitive analysis.

Further, the reaction time was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity changes over time after the NO2 - solution has been added. Fig. 2b shows after adding 2 equiv. of NO2 -, the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm rapidly increased to reach a plateau within about 6 min, implying that NBDA is suitable for fast detecting of NO2 -.

Under the optimized conditions, absorption and fluorescence titration studies were performed. Fig. 3a shows upon the addition of increasing NO2 -, the absorption peak at 477 nm gradually blue shifted to 390 nm with a marked isosbestic point at 418 nm, indicating that only one stable product was formed upon reaction with NO2 -. The absorbance ratio at 390 and 477 nm (A390 nm/A477 nm) exhibits a good linear relationship with the concentration of NO2 - up to 14 μM (R2 = 0.9728) (Fig. 3c), enabling a ratiometric colorimetric assay of NO2 -. According to the definition of three times the standard deviation of the blank signal (S/N = 3), the detection limit was determined to be 4.921 × 10−8 M.

Fluorescence titration showed that the emission intensity of NBDA at 520 nm (F520 nm) increased with the increasing concentration of NO2 - (Fig. 3b), showing a good linear relationship with the NO2 - concentration in the range of 0 ∼ 60 μM (R2 = 0.9924) (Fig. 3d). The detection limit for NO2 - was calculated to be 5.83 × 10−9 M, which is sufficiently low to permit a sensitive quantification of nitrite in water and food samples (WHO, 2008).

Compared with some recently reported NO2 - sensors ( Table 1), NBDA possesses the advantages of facile preparation, dual-mode output, “turn-on” fluorescent responses with good sensitivity, and convenient real-time monitoring through smartphone imaging, which makes NBDA an attractive candidate for detecting of NO2 - in real water and food samples.

Job’s plot derived from fluorescence titration revealed a 1:1 binding stoichiometry between NBDA and NO2 - (Fig.S1). To further elucidate the interaction mechanism of NBDA with NO2 -, 1H NMR studies were performed. As shown in Fig. 4, the characteristic -NH signal (10.7 ppm) of NBDA was found to disappear upon addition of nitrite. Meanwhile, the signals of the aromatic protons (Ha∼Hf) all displayed significant downfield shifts, suggesting the formation of electron-withdrawing triazole moiety. This was further confirmed by electrospray ionization mass spectrometry. The predominant peak at m/z 283.0593 in the MS spectrum of NBDA upon addition of NO2 - was attributed to the triazole product (C12H9N5O+, [M +H] +, calculated data: 283.0580) ( Fig. 5). Thus, it was reasonably concluded that the formation of triazole product is responsible for the optical signal changes induced by NO2 -.

NBDA can identify NO2 − ions by the visible color change and hence, a smartphone-assisted sensing system was developed as a portable detector for the sample analysis by colorimetry ( Fig. 6). Specifically, filter paper was cut into strips (1 cm × 2 cm). After being immersed in DMSO solution containing 1.0 mM NBDA for 5 min, the filter paper strips were taken out and dried in the air. The as-prepared paper strips were sprayed with hydrochloric acid (pH = 1.0), exposed to different concentrations of NO2 - for 10 min, and then were photographed by the smartphone camera under daylight and 365 nm UV lamp, respectively. Fig. 6b shows the color of the paper strip gradually changed from yellow to colorless with increasing concentration of NO2 - and meanwhile the green emission was enhanced under the excitation of UV light from the UV lamp. To realize on-site, sensitive and accurate analysis of the images that have been taken, a color recognizer app, namely Color Assist installed in the smartphone was used to obtain their corresponding RGB (red, green, and blue) values (Fig. 6a). As depicted in Fig. 6c and d, the ratio of R/(R + G + B) values obtained from the daylight images and fluorescence images exhibited a linear relationship with NO2 - concentration in the range of 0–4 × 10−4 M. Consequently, the ratio of R / (R + G + B) values can be used as a parameter for the quantification of NO2 -. The LOD was calculated to be 0.933 μM and 0.521 μM, respectively. Thus, the smartphone-based sensing platform could be potentially used as a portable and instrument-free method for convenient and reliable determination of NO2 - through conducting color change assays, although the sensitivity of which is not great as compared with absorbance or fluorescence intensity measurements.

To verify the feasibility of the proposed method to detect nitrite in actual samples, NBDA was applied to the fluorescence analysis of food samples, including ham sausage and pickle. Food sample preparation details are provided in the Supporting Information. Based on the established standard curve equation, a spiked recovery test was performed with these samples before and after spiking with NO2 - ion solution. The spiked recovery was determined according to Recovery (%) = (measured value of spiked sample - measured value of sample)/ measured value of spiked sample× 100. The results were listed in Table 2. Good recovery percentages (96.13–108.19 %) and low SD values (below 3.5 %) signified good reproducibility and reliability of the proposed method for detection of NO2 - in real samples. In addition, the accuracy of the proposed method for the determination of nitrite in complex matrices was validated by comparison with the established spectrophotometric method (Zhang et al., 2019). The data listed in Table 2 indicated that there was no significant difference between the results determined by the two methods. Hence, NBDA offers great potential for the sensitive and accurate detection of nitrite in food.

Based on the reaction of o-phenylenediamine with NO2 - to form benzotriazole derivatives, a simple NBD-based probe, NBDA, was developed for colorimetric and fluorescent dual detection of NO2 -. The response of the probe to NO2 - in an acidic DMF aqueous solution exhibited a pronounced blue shift in the absorption maximum along with a significant fluorescence enhancement at 520 nm. NBDA was used for the quantitative detection of nitrite in real food samples (including ham sausage and pickle) with satisfactory results. More significantly, a smartphone-based sample analysis by colorimetry was established for reliable visualization and quantitative detection of NO2 - in water samples. With the advantages of facile preparation, dual-mode output, “turn-on” fluorescent response, high sensitivity and selectivity as well as convenient real-time monitoring, NBDA could be an attractive candidate for detecting NO2 - in real water and food samples.

Jianhua Wang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Jingying Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yu Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Resources. Shaomin Shuang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087 and No. 22274090) and the Shanxi Province Hundred Talents Project for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2023.105179.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Shen et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Practical assay for nitrite and nitrosothiol as an alternative to the griess assay or the 2,3-diaminonaphthalene assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shen, Y., Zhang, Q., Qian, X., Y. Yang, 2015. Practical assay for nitrite and nitrosothiol as an alternative to the griess assay or the 2,3-diaminonaphthalene assay. Anal. Chem. 87, 1270–1274.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: O. S-nitroso-N-acetylcysteine generates less carcinogenic N-nitrosamines in meat products than nitrite

RSC Adv. 7, 32769–32776.
